label: Environmental justice
headline: The communities carrying the toxic burden are not the communities capturing the digital gains.
body: The health and environmental costs of e-waste fall on low-income urban communities, informal workers, and families living near dumpsites rather than on the markets where devices were designed, sold, and consumed.
points: Toxic externalities are displaced from Global North consumption markets to Nigerian disposal sites.
Within Nigeria, the burden lands hardest on low-income urban communities.
Groundwater contamination links the digital economy directly to environmental injustice.

label: Labour rights
headline: Informal recovery keeps the system running, but workers remain largely unprotected.
body: Up to 100,000 workers operate without meaningful labour protections, occupational health coverage, social security, or safety equipment even as they do the most dangerous recovery work.
points: Manual dismantling, acid baths, and open burning take place without adequate protection.
Formalisation requires more than registration; it requires enforceable worker protections.
The cooperative-led EPR model will fail if labour rights remain an afterthought.

label: Children's rights
headline: The same digital transition meant to expand opportunity is also creating conditions that kill children.
body: Mortality findings around major e-waste sites show that the burden is not abstract. It is reflected in neonatal and infant deaths, groundwater contamination, and continued concern over child labour in informal recycling.
points: Children near major sites face measurable mortality risk increases.
Residential exposure compounds risks beyond the worksite itself.
Child labour remains insufficiently documented, not safely absent.

label: Digital sovereignty
headline: Nigeria pays the disposal cost of a hardware system it does not control.
body: The country imports the devices, carries the end-of-life burden, and captures only a fraction of the embedded material value while product design and lifespan decisions are made elsewhere.
points: Hardware dependence makes waste dependence a digital policy issue.
Global governance remains asymmetric when EPR fees stay in exporting markets.
A sustainable digital economy requires control over the material lifecycle, not only the software layer.
